Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.

Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1 has a black main area with a blue flower object (top-right), a white cross (bottom-left), a gray dotted diagonal path connecting them, two purple 3x3 squares (top-center in the charcoal bar, bottom-left near the cross), and a charcoal top bar + white bottom bar. The goal likely involves interacting with these objects along the path.
- Goal model: Move purple squares along the dotted path toward the blue flower object. Each click advances a purple square to the next position.
- Plan: Click the next gray dot upward along the path from the purple square to move it further toward the blue flower. The next dot up is at (51, 12).
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The purple square moved from (52-54, 9-11) to (50-52, 11-13). It moved up along the path by 2 rows and 2 cols. So clicking the next gray dot moves the purple square to that position.
The path dots go: (57,6) → (55,8) → cross position → (51,12) → (49,14) → (47,16) → ... → blue flower
Now the purple square is at (50-52, 11-13). I need to continue moving it along the path toward the blue flower. Each step moves it 2 rows up and 2 cols right.
Let me figure out the full path and batch all remaining clicks. The path dots from the segmentation were:
- (57,6), (55,8) - below original cross
- Cross was at ~53,10
- (51,12), (49,14), (47,16), (45,18), (43,20), (41,22), (39,24), (37,26), (35,28), (33,30), (31,32), (29,34), (27,36), (25,38), (23,40), (21,42), (19,44), (17,46)
- Then the blue flower starts around row 17-19, col 44-52
The purple square is now at (50-52, 11-13). The next dots along the path are:
(49,14), (47,16), (45,18), (43,20), (41,22), (39,24), (37,26), (35,28), (33,30), (31,32), (29,34), (27,36), (25,38), (23,40), (21,42), (19,44), (17,46)
That's 17 more clicks to reach the blue flower. But wait - the target says about 22 actions for level 1. Let me think about whether there might be more mechanics.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe I should also move the top purple square somehow. Or maybe the goal is to get both purple squares to the blue flower?
For now, let me batch the clicks along the path to move this purple square all the way to the blue flower. The next click should be at (49, 14).
Let me batch several clicks at once.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The path dots going upward from current position:
# Current purple square center ~ (51, 12)
# Next dots: (49,14), (47,16), (45,18), (43,20), (41,22), (39,24), (37,26), (35,28), (33,30), (31,32), (29,34), (27,36), (25,38), (23,40), (21,42), (19,44), (17,46)
# The blue flower is at rows 11-19, cols 44-52

# Let me batch all remaining path clicks
path_dots = [
    (49, 14), (47, 16), (45, 18), (43, 20), (41, 22),
    (39, 24), (37, 26), (35, 28), (33, 30), (31, 32),
    (29, 34), (27, 36), (25, 38), (23, 40), (21, 42),
    (19, 44), (17, 46)
]

actions = [{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': r, 'col': c} for r, c in path_dots]
result = action(actions)
print(f"Batch result: {result}")

# Check final state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nFinal purple objects:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'p':
        print(f"  id={node['id']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")

print(f"\nLevel completed: {result.get('level_completed', False)}")
print(f"Game over: {result.get('game_over', False)}")
print(f"Done: {result.get('done', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
